scriptId: cs_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
historyId: h_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Excessive phone or social media use

memories.content: User intends to reduce excessive phone and social media use and will share context gradually over several messages to receive a final practical plan; Assistant agrees to proceed, inviting the first bit of context and stating a practical plan will be created after all context is provided.

User asked for broad frameworks to reduce phone/social use; assistant recommended a mix of four frameworks—habit substitution, strict limits, environmental tweaks, and behavioral design/friction—describing for each the “What,” when it’s best, pros, cons, and providing an example for habit substitution; recommended combining approaches: start with environmental tweaks and friction for quick, low-effort wins, layer habit substitutions for times you’d normally use the phone, and use strict limits selectively (e.g., deep work, evenings); offered to tailor a concrete mix by learning when and why the user uses their phone.

User asks for small, sustainable changes that fit into everyday life instead of an all-or-nothing detox; assistant agrees to prioritize small, low-friction changes and asks for the next context when ready.

In this interaction, a user asked whether replacing urges with another activity is better than using strict timers and gates. The assistant replied that both strategies can work and effectiveness depends on why the phone is used and how strong the habit/urge

The user asked to perform text compression on the provided string; the assistant supplied a condensed version with sections on Morning routines, Evening routines, App placement and home screen tweaks, Notification rules, Low-effort behavioral tactics, Settings and small tech tweaks, a Quick one-week setup checklist, and an offer to turn three checklist items into a two-step start plan, detailing specific items under each section.

User stated a preference for non-judgmental, supportive guidance rather than scolding; assistant agreed to maintain a non-judgmental and supportive approach, use encouraging language, emphasize small wins and practical steps, avoid scolding, and be ready for the next bit of context.

One speaker stated they will add important specifics and constraints in subsequent messages to allow adapting the plan; the other speaker agreed to adapt the plan accordingly once those specifics and constraints are provided.

User stated that most problematic use is on a few social apps they check constantly (short-video and photo/text feeds). Assistant provided concrete steps to reduce usage: set daily limits for top 1–2 apps (15–45 minutes); move/offscreen those apps; turn off notifications and badges and lock-screen previews; disable autoplay and Background App Refresh; enable grayscale/reduce motion; replace the app with a browser bookmark for occasional use; schedule social time (one short, intentional session); implement a one-check rule with a 60-second pause; and stated these would be folded into a two-week trial plan when ready.

The user asked to compress a string containing a plan to cap social-media use at 90 minutes per day; the assistant provided a detailed implementation guide covering quick setup (OS limits, category/app timers, exempting calls/texts), three usage structures (batched, micro-sessions, single 90-minute session), behavioral/environmental tweaks (disable notifications, move apps off home screen, disable autoplay/refresh, use grayscale), pre-decided override rules (how many overrides, passcode use), tracking and gamification (daily quick check, simple
User stated that weekday screen time averages about four hours and feels too high; assistant suggested choosing between a batched schedule (3×30 min) or micro-sessions for a 90-minute social app cap.
